title: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.7.10 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.7.10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.23: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.23' running on Sysmon EventCode=22, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=endpoint sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational EventCode=22 earliest=-30d
| lookup restricted_subnets.csv src_ip OUTPUT zone
| where zone="engineering_secure"
| stats count by ComputerName, Image
description: Uses endpoint logs for removable media mounts in restricted VLANs to enforce media handling procedures.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes endpoint and sourcetypes XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
implementation: (1) USB policy allowlist; (2) DLP correlation optional; (3) quarterly sample for auditors. Indexes required: `index=endpoint`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational`; Lookups: `restricted_subnets`, `restricted_subnets.csv`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Sysmon or CrowdStrike.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Sysmon EventCode=22.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) USB policy allowlist; (2) DLP correlation optional; (3) quarterly sample for auditors.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=endpoint sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational EventCode=22 earliest=-30d
| lookup restricted_subnets.csv src_ip OUTPUT zone
| where zone="engineering_secure"
| stats count by ComputerName, Image
```

Understanding this SPL

**Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations (A.7.10)** — Uses endpoint logs for removable media mounts in restricted VLANs to enforce media handling procedures.

Documented **Data sources**: Sysmon EventCode=22. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Sysmon or CrowdStrike. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: endpoint; **sourcetype**: XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=endpoint, sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where zone="engineering_secure"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by ComputerName, Image** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Bar chart
knownFalsePositives: Incidents may bunch during major events, vulnerability bulletin response, or controlled exercises.
